response: The EU’s General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR), effective May 2018, applies to any company processing the personal data of EU residents—wherever it’s based—and grants rights to data access, erasure, portability, and requires explicit consent, appointment of Data Protection Officers for large‐scale processing, and breach notification within 72 hours.  Non-compliance can trigger fines of up to 4% of global annual revenue or €20 million (whichever is higher); notable penalties include Meta’s €1.2 billion fine in 2023, Amazon’s €746 million, and over €200 million levied against Google.  GDPR has inspired similar laws worldwide—such as California’s CCPA, Brazil’s LGPD, and India’s Digital Personal Data Protection Act—but critics warn it imposes costly compliance burdens on small businesses, stifles innovation, and floods users with cookie‐consent banners amid uneven enforcement.  Despite these challenges, GDPR enshrined data privacy as a fundamental right and set the global benchmark for data protection legislation.
